None of the options directly addresses the scenario in which the operator publicly frames platform-neutral fallback as a security threat to citizen trust, forcing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to choose between incumbent operator reassurance and project credibility — a choice likely to favor the incumbent.

levers.name: Demonstrator Feature Scope and Vendor-Lock Risk Narrative
levers.consequences: Phase two demonstrators can be scoped narrowly (authentication only, no lifecycle management or revocation UI) or broadly (including account binding, credential renewal, emergency revocation interfaces). Narrow scope produces faster, more defensible research; broad scope produces evidence that fallback systems are usable in practice, not just technically feasible. However, broad scope requires expanded team capacity (additional UX researcher, security review depth) and invites mission creep toward a quasi-product. The narrative choice is whether demonstrators are positioned as architecture proofs (vendor-lock risk is structural) or usability proofs (vendor-lock risk is avoidable through good design).
options: Limit phase two demonstrators strictly to authentication handshake and token validation (FIDO2/WebAuthn to OpenID Connect bridge), excluding account binding, revocation UI, and lifecycle operations; position the work as architectural proof that platform-neutral authentication is feasible, leaving usability hardening to production phases.

Develop two complete mini-systems including authentication, credential binding, emergency revocation flows, and user consent interfaces, creating end-to-end evidence that fallback authentication is not only feasible but usable; accept expanded timeline and cost pressure in exchange for stronger public and procurement credibility.

Build a single end-to-end demonstrator covering authentication and revocation, plus a separate minimalist architectural reference document that vendors could use to implement the pattern independently, splitting the credibility burden between live evidence and standards-articulation.
levers.review: The tension is between speed and credibility: narrow scope is defensible as research but risks being dismissed by procurement teams as 'not real'; broad scope produces credibility but requires team skill depth (UX, accessibility, security review) that may not be available within the 10.5M DKK budget and timeline. None of the options directly addresses what happens if mid-phase two the demonstrator uncovers a significant usability or security problem that would require scope expansion — the project then faces a choice between delaying delivery or shipping demonstrators with known gaps.

levers.name: Publication Strategy and Evidence Accessibility for Policy Uptake
levers.consequences: The project's influence on AltID and EU standards depends on how feasibility reports, demonstrator code, and architectural specifications are published and communicated. Publishing only peer-reviewed journal articles creates credibility with academics but delays reach (12–18 month publication cycles) and limits immediate policy impact; publishing rapidly as working papers or policy briefs accelerates government uptake but risks charges of insufficient rigor; open-sourcing demonstrator code builds practitioner credibility but may be perceived as competing with incumbent operators or as a commitment to product maintenance. The tension is between academic rigor, policy timeliness, and operational sustainability.
options: Publish the feasibility report and architectural specifications as rapid-access policy briefs (month 7, month 19) through Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or a partner think tank, reserve detailed technical work for peer-reviewed venues, and open-source demonstrator code only under a restrictive license that prevents commercial use without explicit coordination with the project.

Publish all work (feasibility report, demonstrator code, architectural specifications) as open-source and pre-print materials within 1–2 weeks of completion, prioritizing rapid accessibility and enabling immediate policy and practitioner uptake even at the cost of formal peer review lag, and license code under permissive terms (MIT/Apache 2.0) to maximize adoption.

Coordinate with a Scandinavian or European digital-identity research consortium to publish findings through a combination of peer-reviewed journals, consortium policy reports, and restricted-access technical specifications shared only with authorized government and standards bodies, balancing academic credibility with policy confidentiality.
levers.review: The tension is between urgency and credibility: rapid publication accelerates policy uptake but invites criticism that the work has not undergone sufficient external security review before influencing government procurement language; peer-reviewed publication takes 12–18 months but arrives too late to influence AltID design windows; consortium publication splits the difference but introduces coordination overhead and potential conflicts between consortium members. None of the options directly addresses what happens if the demonstrators or feasibility report contain findings that are unflattering to Danish government digital-identity strategy — whether the project should suppress or reframe them to preserve institutional relationships, or publish them fully and risk being excluded from future advisory roles.

levers.name: Fallback-Authentication Trust Model and Assurance Level
levers.consequences: Choosing whether the fallback path must achieve full eIDAS2 'Substantial' level or can initially operate at a lower assurance tier directly determines technical scope, certification burden, and timeline. A lower entry point reduces phase two cost and accelerates institutional traction, but risks permanent relegation to non-critical use cases; full 'Substantial' parity positions the fallback as a genuine alternative, but multiplies demonstrator complexity and delays deployment signal.
options: Design the fallback demonstrator to achieve eIDAS2 'Substantial' assurance from the outset, targeting full architectural parity with MitID's trust posture and pursuing external certification to establish production credibility.

Implement the fallback at eIDAS2 'High' level initially, accepting that early-phase deployment would be confined to lower-risk transactions, with a roadmap to 'Substantial' certification in a follow-on phase.

Build a phased fallback architecture where a minimal 'High' assurance core (e.g. FIDO2 over hardware key) is immediately available, while delegating higher-assurance scenarios to persistent platform-OS attestation until platform dependencies can be architected out entirely.
levers.review: Assurance-level parity versus timeline speed creates a false choice — but none of the options address the underlying institutional problem: Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator may classify any non-platform-mediated fallback as inherently 'lower assurance' regardless of cryptographic strength, because assurance frameworks implicitly trust platform vendors' attestation chains. Architectural parity alone will not overcome that institutional bias without explicit policy change, which is a separate lever.

levers.name: Demonstrator Technical Footprint and Vendor-Neutral Platform Choice
levers.consequences: Selecting GrapheneOS, Calyx OS, or a custom Linux environment for the mobile demonstrator determines both technical defensibility and institutional credibility. GrapheneOS offers maximum proprietary-OS deviation while maintaining Android compatibility; Calyx OS balances community governance with mainstream recognition; Linux isolates the demonstrator entirely from the duopoly but sacrifices usability validation. Each choice signals a different message about feasibility and real-world viability.
options: Deploy the demonstrator exclusively on GrapheneOS, emphasizing maximum vendor independence from Google, with explicit documentation that the platform provides equivalent or superior integrity and revocation capabilities without reliance on Google Play Services.

Implement the demonstrator across both Calyx OS and a reference build of the AOSP (Android Open Source Project) base, positioning the work as compatible with community-maintained Android forks and available to users who reject proprietary Google services.

Develop two parallel demonstrators — a mobile implementation on a minimalist Linux environment (e.g. PinePhone Pro with custom authentication stack) and a separate browser-plus-hardware-token fallback on commodity laptops, side-stepping the mobile OS duopoly entirely for the primary fallback pathway.
levers.review: Platform choice implicitly claims something about realistic adoption and institutional acceptance. GrapheneOS legitimacy is growing but remains niche; Calyx OS and AOSP forks are fragmented in user base; Linux phones are credible for security research but distant from mainstream Danish digital-service usage patterns. None of the options reconcile the tension between technical purity and institutional persuasiveness — the demonstrator may be architecturally sound yet politically invisible if it cannot demonstrate that ordinary citizens (or at least sympathetic civil servants) can realistically use it for real public-sector tasks.

levers.name: EU Standards-Alignment and Wallet-Framework Positioning
levers.consequences: Timing and depth of engagement with the European Digital Identity Wallet working groups and eIDAS2 technical committees determines whether Danish requirements can influence the EU's own platform-neutrality and fallback-access guidance before member states begin AltID-equivalent implementations. Early, credible input shapes standards; late arrival means adapting to already-hardened EU consensus. Success here creates external legitimacy and reduces the political isolation of Danish demands.
options: Secure formal observer or working-group participation in the European Digital Identity Wallet technical committees by month 10, positioning the project's feasibility findings and demonstrator insights as direct input to EU-level architecture and interoperability specifications.

Publish the project's feasibility and demonstrator results in peer-reviewed and open-access channels by month 18, submitting them to ETSI, CEN/CENELEC working groups and EU Commission consultation processes as evidence-based commentary on platform neutrality and fallback resilience.

Establish bilateral relationships with 3–5 EU member states (e.g. Sweden, Germany, Netherlands) that share sovereignty and resilience concerns, jointly submitting a member-state letter to the European Commission advocating for platform-neutral and fallback requirements in the wallet framework, amplifying Danish voice through alliance.
levers.review: EU engagement assumes that Brussels will welcome member-state input on supplier-concentration and digital-sovereignty concerns. In practice, the Commission may treat platform-neutral mandates as friction impeding rapid wallet rollout, or may defer to incumbent operators' arguments about implementation cost. None of the options includes a back-up strategy for when EU consensus rejects the platform-neutrality framing; if the wallet framework hardens around Apple/Google integration, Danish influence becomes moot regardless of technical and coalition quality.

levers.name: Coalition Depth and Contingency-Planning Constituency
levers.consequences: Recruiting contingency-planning authorities, telecommunications regulators, and critical-infrastructure resilience stakeholders into the coalition broadens political pressure beyond privacy advocates and adds institutional weight from entities formally responsible for supply-chain and continuity risk. This reframes the issue from 'privacy zealots versus innovation' to 'risk managers versus unmitigated single-point-of-failure.' Success here shifts burden of proof onto operators to justify why continuity planning does not demand fallback capacity.
options: Prioritize formal coalition participation by the Danish Emergency Management Agency (Beredskabsstyrelsen), critical-infrastructure regulatory bodies, and the telecommunications industry association, positioning platform-neutral access explicitly as continuity-of-service resilience rather than privacy.

Build coalition membership around academic cybersecurity and resilience research centers, large Danish enterprises and public-sector IT procurement officers who can articulate first-hand experience with vendor concentration and switching costs, creating peer-driven institutional pressure.

Recruit sympathetic members of Parlamentarisk Revisionsudvalget (Parliamentary Audit Committee) and regional authorities (kommuner) who manage public digital-service delivery, enabling the coalition to frame the issue through governance and fiscal-accountability lenses rather than security and privacy alone.
levers.review: Contingency-planning framing is powerful but masks an uncomfortable tension: genuine resilience often requires accepting temporary service degradation or reduced convenience during fallback scenarios. None of the options address how to price in the actual cost and user friction of a platform-neutral fallback path, or how to build coalition consensus around accepting degraded UX as the price of sovereignty. If convenient solutions exist, institutional actors will avoid the complexity — and any fallback that requires slightly slower authentication or additional user steps will be rejected as unacceptable even if technically robust.

levers.name: Incumbent-Operator Engagement and Resistance-Softening Approach
levers.consequences: The strategy for engaging MitID's operator (Statens IT or similar authority) determines whether the project is framed as collaborative resilience planning or adversarial pressure. Early, constructive engagement can neutralize opposition and unlock internal advocates; public campaigning without prior dialogue hardens institutional defensiveness and risks being dismissed as external interference. Timing of disclosure matters enormously.
options: Establish a formal technical working relationship with the MitID operator's architecture and security teams starting in phase one, positioning the project as independent research supporting their own resilience planning, with explicit commitments to confidentiality regarding operational details while maintaining public freedom to publish feasibility findings.

Conduct all phase one and phase two work in parallel without formal operator engagement, then present completed demonstrators and feasibility findings as an external perspective, positioning the project as peer review and resilience challenge rather than collaboration, forcing the operator to respond publicly to technical and policy proposals.

Engage the MitID operator only after phase two demonstrators are complete and published, approaching them with concrete technical evidence and coalition support, negotiating explicitly for formal acknowledgment of fallback-access requirements in AltID planning in exchange for project visibility and institutional neutrality.
levers.review: All three options assume the MitID operator will ultimately cooperate once presented with sufficient evidence or pressure. But if the operator's institutional mandate and vendor relationships make platform-neutral fallback genuinely incompatible with their operating model, technical credibility and coalition size become irrelevant. None of the options include a contingency for genuine, principled institutional opposition — or a strategy for working around the operator if necessary, e.g., through direct procurement language in AltID governance documents that bypass the existing operator's consent.

levers.name: Procurement-Language Intervention and AltID Governance Timing
levers.consequences: Inserting supplier-neutral and fallback-access requirements into AltID procurement documents before the request-for-proposal phase is published gives vendors time to design-in compliance; insertion during or after RFP release forces costly redesign or compliance failure. Phase three timing (months 17–24) is late but may be realistic given institutional planning cycles. Success here locks in requirements for years; failure means the next identity stack repeats the current supplier concentration.
options: Secure a formal review slot in AltID's architecture and requirements workshop (target: month 14), presenting the demonstrators and feasibility findings to procurement and technical stakeholders, aiming to influence the RFP draft before formal publication.

Coordinate with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's procurement team to include platform-neutrality and fallback-access language in the final RFP published in month 18–20, ensuring that vendor bids must address the requirements rather than treating them as optional enhancements.

Prepare a formal amendment proposal (juridisk tillæg) to the AltID governance framework and procurement terms, leveraging coalition and EU-level support to submit the proposal through official channels for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's consideration, effectively forcing a formal institutional response regardless of enthusiasm.
levers.review: Procurement language sounds concrete, but AltID governance is complex and fragmented across multiple authorities and existing vendor contracts. None of the options address the political economy of existing relationships — if incumbent operators or major platform vendors lobby behind closed doors, public procurement language will be watered down, interpreted narrowly, or buried in non-binding 'guidance' rather than enforced as hard requirements. Timing matters less than institutional will, which the project cannot guarantee.

levers.name: Risk-Narrative Framing and Geopolitical Contingency Messaging
levers.consequences: Communicating the project's core message — platform-vendor concentration as a continuity-of-service and sovereignty risk — can emphasize resilience, European autonomy, or democratic accountability, each resonating differently with different stakeholders. The chosen narrative shapes coalition membership, institutional receptiveness, and sustainability if political winds shift. Geopolitical framing risks polarization; resilience framing achieves broader buy-in but may deprioritize urgency.
options: Emphasize the geopolitical and strategic-autonomy dimensions explicitly, framing platform dependency as a vulnerability to U.S. regulatory overreach or extraterritorial enforcement (e.g. CFIUS or sanctions), positioning platform neutrality as sovereignty protection for Denmark and the EU.

Lead with resilience and continuity-of-service messaging, highlighting the concrete risk of service interruption if Apple or Google change app-store policies, revoke authentication certificates, or experience regional outages, positioning the fallback as a businesslike contingency measure.

Blend narrative across different audiences — geopolitical framing for Folketinget and EU engagements, resilience framing for public-sector IT buyers and contingency planners, and democratic-accountability framing for civil-society partners — ensuring the coalition message remains cohesive while tailoring delivery.
levers.review: Narrative multiplicity creates an internal tension: if the project simultaneously claims that this is about sovereignty, resilience, and democracy, it risks appearing unfocused or instrumentally exploiting multiple concerns rather than addressing a genuine problem. A single, crisp narrative is more persuasive but may alienate coalition members with different primary motivations. The chosen framing also invites incumbent-operator and platform-vendor counter-narratives — if the project overemphasizes geopolitical risk, vendors will claim the project is anti-American or ideological rather than technical; if it emphasizes resilience, vendors will counter that modern cloud infrastructure is already resilient enough. No framing is immune to rebuttal.

You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
